concept_name: Head-to-Tail Vector Addition — The Geometric Rule
source_book: DC Pandey Ch.5.4 (Triangle Law of Vector Addition / geometric methods) — table-of-contents reference only; teaching authored from first principles. Phase 0 validation demo Sim 1 (session 56). Re-anchored to displacement after round-1 review (rain-umbrella application moved out — already covered by umbrella_tilt_angle.json).

real_world_anchor.primary: You walk 3 metres east to the tea stall, then 4 metres north to your friend's house. You walked 7 metres of path total — but you end up only 5 metres away from where you started, on a slanted line going north-east. The 7 (path you walked) and the 5 (how far you ended up) are different things. This concept teaches HOW TO COMPUTE that 5 — and its direction — using the head-to-tail rule for vectors. Once this rule clicks, projectile motion, relative motion, and force balance all become geometry problems.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Two students pulling a box with ropes — one pulls 3 newtons east, the other pulls 4 newtons north. The box doesn't move 7 newtons in any direction. It moves 5 newtons at an angle, north-east. Same head-to-tail rule, applied to forces instead of displacements.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: Ch.6 will apply this rule to relative motion (rain-umbrella, river-boat). Ch.7 to projectile motion. Ch.8 to net force on a body. The rule itself is what we learn here, in pure form, before any of those applications.

formulas.head_to_tail_rule: Place tail of second vector at head of first; resultant = arrow from start of first to tip of second
formulas.resultant_magnitude: |R| = sqrt(d_east^2 + d_north^2) when d_east and d_north are perpendicular

constraints: Head-to-tail rule applies to ANY two vectors regardless of orientation; this concept demonstrates with perpendicular legs (east + north) for clarity
Resultant is the SHORTCUT — the diagonal of the chain, not the sum of the leg lengths
Order of addition does not change the resultant (commutativity); chain shape changes but endpoint stays the same
Vectors must be drawn to the same scale for the geometric construction to be meaningful

STATE_1.title: Walked 3 East + 4 North — How Far From Start?

scene_composition.text: You walk 3 m east, then 4 m north. How far are you from where you started?
Start (home)
3 m east
4 m north
End (friend's house)

Guess A:
3 + 4 = 7 m

Guess B:
Less than 7
(you turned)

Guess C:
Exactly 5 m,
on a slanted line
Path = 7 m walked. End-point distance = 5 m. The head-to-tail rule tells us exactly why.

tts_sentences.text_en: You walk three metres east to the tea stall, then four metres north to your friend's house.
Total walking — three plus four — is seven metres of path.
But how far is your friend's house from your home? Not seven. It's five metres, on a slanted line going north-east.
Path walked is seven. End-point distance is five. They are different. The head-to-tail rule tells us why and gives us the exact answer.

scene_composition.text: Two displacement vectors. Each has a length and a direction.

Two arrows. Different lengths, different directions, different starting points.
Question — how do we add them?

tts_sentences.text_en: Strip away the story. We just have two displacement vectors.
Vector A — three metres east. One arrow, pointing right.
Vector B — four metres north. Another arrow, pointing up.
Two arrows. Different lengths, different directions, drawn at different starting points. How do we add them to find the resultant?

scene_composition.text: STATE 3 — The Head-to-Tail Rule
A = 3 m east
B = 4 m north

Tail-of-second touches
HEAD-of-first.
This is the rule.
Chain complete: A → B (east first, then north)

tts_sentences.text_en: Two vectors. We need to add them. Here's the rule every physicist uses.
Take the second vector — vector B, four metres north. Pick it up by its tail.
Where do you think we should put B's tail?
Place its tail at the HEAD of vector A. Tip to tail. That's the rule.
The two vectors now form a chain. East three, then north four — connected, head to tail.

misconception_watch.belief: Both vectors must start from the same point
misconception_watch.one_line_fix: No — head-to-tail means TAIL of one touches HEAD of the other, never tail-to-tail.

STATE_4.title: The Resultant — Five Metres on a Diagonal

scene_composition.text: STATE 4 — The Resultant
A (3 m)
B (4 m)
R = A + B (the resultant)
From the START of the chain to the TIP of the chain. This diagonal is your displacement: 5 m on a slanted line.

tts_sentences.text_en: We've stacked the arrows. Now where's the resultant — your end-point distance from the start?
Trace from the start of vector A. Where do you think the resultant ends?
From the start of the chain to the tip of the chain. That diagonal is the resultant.
By Pythagoras — three squared plus four squared equals twenty-five — the diagonal is exactly five metres long. That's your end-point distance.

STATE_5.title: Try It — Drag the East Distance

scene_composition.text: STATE 5 — Your Turn
A = east (drag below)
B = 4 m north (fixed)
R = resultant
d_east = 1 → R is almost vertical (you barely walked east)
d_east = 8 → R is almost horizontal (you barely walked north)

tts_sentences.text_en: Your turn. Drag the slider for the east distance. Watch the resultant change.
Push it down. Resultant tips upward, almost vertical, because the east leg shrinks.
Push it up. Resultant lies almost horizontal, because the east leg now dominates over the four-metre north leg.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Adding the leg lengths numerically — 3 + 4 = 7 m as the end-point distance
trigger_phrases: resultant equals A plus B as numbers

STATE_1.title: The Wrong Belief — Just Add the Numbers

scene_composition.text: MYTH: End-point distance = 3 + 4 = 7 m

If 7 m were the end-point distance, you'd land 7 m east of home — but you walked NORTH for the second leg.
7 m east of home is NOT your friend's house.

tts_sentences.text_en: Many students reflexively answer seven — three plus four.
But seven in what direction? If your end-point is seven metres from home, in what direction is it?
There's no consistent direction that makes sense. Seven metres east is not your friend's house. The naive sum can't be the answer.

STATE_2.title: Why Numerical Addition Fails — Direction Carries Information

scene_composition.text:
Numbers (scalars) only carry MAGNITUDE.
Displacement vectors also carry DIRECTION.

Adding 3 m east to 4 m north by simple arithmetic ignores the directions completely.
The answer must respect both magnitudes AND both directions.

That's why we use the head-to-tail rule.
The geometry encodes the directions.

tts_sentences.text_en: Numbers only carry magnitude. Displacement vectors also carry direction.
Adding three-east and four-north by simple arithmetic ignores the directions completely.
The right answer respects both magnitudes AND both directions — that's why we use the head-to-tail rule. The geometry encodes the directions.

STATE_3.title: The Correct Number — Exactly 5 m on a Slanted Line

scene_composition.text:
Right triangle:
  east leg  = A = 3
  north leg = B = 4
  diagonal = R = end-point distance

|R|² = 3² + 4² = 9 + 16 = 25
|R|  = √25 = 5 m

Not 7. Exactly 5.

Gap: 7 − 5 = 2 m
That's the entire reason vectors exist as a concept distinct from numbers.
Branch 1 — Final answer: 5 m

tts_sentences.text_en: Compute the correct answer. East leg three, north leg four — they form a right triangle.
By Pythagoras the diagonal is the square root of nine plus sixteen — square root of twenty-five — exactly five metres.
Five, not seven. The two-metre gap is the entire reason vectors exist as a concept distinct from numbers.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Stack both vector tails at the same point and draw a diagonal between heads (parallelogram half-built)
trigger_phrases: both arrows start at same point

scene_composition.text:
MYTH: Stack both arrows from the same starting point
           and draw a diagonal between the heads.

This diagonal connects the two vector HEADS — but it doesn't start at the original starting point.
It's not your end-point displacement.

tts_sentences.text_en: Some students stack both arrows from the same starting point and draw a diagonal between the heads.
But that diagonal doesn't start at your original starting point. It connects the two arrowheads to each other.
It can't be the end-point displacement, because end-point displacement must be measured FROM the starting point, not BETWEEN the two heads.

STATE_2.title: The Correct Move — Slide One Arrow, Keep Direction

scene_composition.text:
FIX: Pick up the SECOND arrow.
         Slide it so its TAIL touches
         the HEAD of the FIRST.
         Direction stays the same.

tts_sentences.text_en: Here's the fix. Don't stack tails. Pick up the SECOND arrow and slide it so its tail touches the HEAD of the first.
Its direction never changes — only its position. That's a parallel translation.
Now draw the diagonal from the start of the chain to the tip. That arrow points correctly — north-east at five metres. That's the end-point displacement.

scene_composition.text:
Two methods give the same answer:

1. Head-to-tail (slide second arrow, draw diagonal)
2. Parallelogram (stack tails, draw diagonal between heads with proper construction)

Head-to-tail extends naturally to 3+ vectors.
Parallelogram doesn't.

Learn head-to-tail. It scales.
Tail-to-tail with a diagonal between heads CAN work — but you must complete the parallelogram correctly. The students above tried half the construction.
Branch 2 — Both methods agree when done right

tts_sentences.text_en: There IS a valid tail-to-tail method called the parallelogram law — but you must complete the parallelogram and draw the diagonal between the heads, not just any diagonal.
The students earlier drew half a parallelogram — wrong direction, wrong diagonal.
Head-to-tail is cleaner because it extends naturally to three or more vectors. Use head-to-tail. It scales.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Drawing the resultant from the chain JUNCTION (head of first = tail of second) instead of from the START
trigger_phrases: draw arrow from head of first

STATE_1.title: The Wrong Belief — Resultant from the Junction

scene_composition.text: MYTH: The resultant starts where the chain joins.

If the resultant started at the junction, it would lie on top of vector B — adding nothing.
The whole chain would be pointless.

tts_sentences.text_en: Some students draw the resultant from the JUNCTION — where the head of one arrow meets the tail of the next.
But that arrow lies on top of the second vector — it adds nothing.
If that were the resultant, the whole chain construction would be pointless.

STATE_2.title: The Correct Move — From the START of the Chain

scene_composition.text:
FIX: Draw from where the FIRST vector STARTED
         to where the LAST vector ENDS.

tts_sentences.text_en: The fix — draw from where the FIRST vector started, all the way to where the LAST vector ends.
That's the SHORTCUT — instead of walking the chain, you take the diagonal.
The resultant is the displacement from start to end of the chain. Always start-to-end, never junction-to-end.

scene_composition.text:
Imagine actually walking the chain:
   step 1: walk along A (3 m east)
   step 2: walk along B (4 m north)
   end up: 4 m north, 3 m east of where you started

The straight line from start to end is the RESULTANT.
It's the SHORTCUT to the same destination.
Crow flies 5 m north-east. You walked 7 m. Same destination.
Branch 3 — Resultant = the shortcut

tts_sentences.text_en: Imagine actually walking the chain — three metres east, then four metres north.
You end up four north and three east of the start. The straight diagonal from start to end is the resultant.
A crow could fly the diagonal — five metres north-east. You walked seven. Same destination, different paths.

epic_c_branches.misconception: Both vectors must be rotated to point in the same direction before they can be added

STATE_1.title: The Wrong Belief — Align First, Then Add

scene_composition.text:
MYTH: I must rotate one vector to align with the other
           before I can add them.

If you rotate A to point north, it's no longer 'walked east' — you've changed its physical meaning.
Now A + B is just '3 north + 4 north = 7 north', which is wrong.

tts_sentences.text_en: Some students think both vectors must point the same direction before they can be added — so they rotate one to match the other.
But if you rotate the east leg to point north, it's no longer 'walked east' — you've changed its physical meaning.
You'd end up adding three-north plus four-north equals seven-north, which is wrong. You can't add a different vector and expect the same answer.

STATE_2.title: The Correct Move — Vectors Add at Any Angle

scene_composition.text:
FIX: Vectors add at ANY angle.
         The head-to-tail rule does NOT require rotation.
         Each vector keeps its OWN direction throughout.

Both vectors keep their original directions.
The HEAD of A meets the TAIL of B — that's all the alignment needed.
The resultant captures BOTH directions automatically.

tts_sentences.text_en: Vectors add at any angle. The head-to-tail rule does not require rotation.
Each vector keeps its own direction throughout — A stays east, B stays north.
The HEAD of A meets the TAIL of B — that's all the alignment needed. The resultant captures both directions automatically.

scene_composition.text:
1D motion only needs numbers (signed scalars).
2D motion needs ARROWS — magnitude AND direction.

Forcing alignment collapses the second dimension.
You'd lose ALL the geometric information.

This is why head-to-tail exists.
It preserves directions while combining magnitudes.

Resolution into components (next concept) flips this:
you TEMPORARILY align with axes for arithmetic, then recombine.
Branch 4 — Why head-to-tail exists

tts_sentences.text_en: One-dimensional motion only needs numbers — signed scalars. Two-dimensional motion needs arrows — magnitude AND direction.
Forcing alignment collapses the second dimension. You'd lose all the geometric information.
That's why head-to-tail exists. It preserves directions while combining magnitudes.

regeneration_variants.label: Same physics, different distances (5 east, 12 north → 13 m diagonal)
regeneration_variants.physical_world: Walk 5 m east, then 12 m north. Resultant = 13 m at arctan(12/5) ≈ 67.4° from east.
regeneration_variants.same_physics: Head-to-tail rule applies identically; the resultant magnitude and angle change but the geometric construction does not. The classic 5-12-13 right-triangle example.

board.note: Stub — board mode for vector_head_to_tail will be authored in Sim 2 (Newton's 2nd law) per session-55 plan. Sim 1 ships conceptual-only.

shortcuts: When two vectors are PERPENDICULAR, |R| = sqrt(a² + b²) (Pythagoras directly).
When two vectors are PARALLEL same direction, |R| = a + b. When ANTIPARALLEL, |R| = |a - b|.
Direction of resultant from one of the legs = arctan(opposite_leg / adjacent_leg) — only valid for perpendicular legs.
edge_cases: Either leg = 0 → resultant equals the other leg exactly.
Both legs equal → resultant = leg × √2 at 45° from either leg.
competitive.note: Competitive-mode states will be authored post-validation if 10-student demo confirms demand for shortcut layer.
